text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 31 May 2013 

Dr Fiona  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Fiona,

Susan Miller, DOB: 14-05-1986, Hospital No: P948572 NHS No. 7623549812  
12 Baker Street, London, NW1 6XE  

Epilepsy 

Medication: Patient-reported use of lamotrigine with variable adherence since retirement. She describes taking Lamotrigine dispersible most mornings but occasionally missing evening doses when she falls asleep in the armchair. Current pack at home is labelled “Lamotrigine dispersible 25 mg,” and she believes she is taking one tablet twice daily; however, there is confusion over prior instructions and no recent written dosing plan was found at home. 

Lamotrigine Blood Level: 

No recent level available; the last community phlebotomy attempt was unsuccessful due to poor venous access and she postponed the repeat owing to transport difficulties. She is agreeable to a re-check locally if transport can be arranged. 

Seizures: She has had five seizures so far this month, seven in Apr, one in Mar and four in Feb, with events reported from both daytime and nocturnal periods. She relates these to a newly retired routine with long solitary days, reduced social contact, and irregular mealtimes. There have been brief post-event confusions but no injuries reported. No consistent auras or reliable warnings are described. 

History/Context: I conducted a scheduled telephone review today. Since retiring from teaching in January, she lives alone and keeps a regular domestic routine with early mornings, a midday walk when the weather permits, and early evenings. Social contact is reduced; she notes that on quieter days she may nap in the afternoon and occasionally forgets her evening dose. She denies alcohol use and reports adequate hydration, though she sometimes skips lunch. Sleep is generally 6–7 hours but fragmented; she is unsure whether some night events disturb her sleep or follow it. She does not drive. 

Plan and Safety: We reviewed medication routines and she agreed to place the evening dose by the kettle as a prompt, and to set phone reminders. She will maintain a simple seizure and trigger diary, including meal timing and sleep duration. I have requested a community blood test for a lamotrigine level and basic profile; we will review results by phone. She is aware of standard seizure first-aid and has a neighbour as an emergency contact. She declined any immediate changes today, preferring to stabilise routines first. I will arrange a follow-up call in six weeks. 

Please do not hesitate to contact me if further information is required. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly lists monthly seizure counts: five so far in May, seven in April, one in March, and four in February. Summing these gives 5 + 7 + 1 + 4 = 17 seizures over the 4-month period from February to May 2013. Therefore, the normalized seizure frequency is 17 per 4 months.
